enrichment.short_description: Crowdcast is a browser-based live video platform for hosting webinars, online courses, summits, and interactive community events with built-in monetization.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Crowdcast is a no-download live video platform for webinars, courses, and summits with built-in ticketing. Compare Crowdcast pricing, features, and pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Crowdcast is a browser-based live video and virtual events platform built for creators, coaches, marketers, and community leaders who host webinars, online courses, summits, and interactive shows. Founded in 2015 and headquartered in Oakland, California, Crowdcast was started by CEO Sai Hossain, who set out to build a way for people to broadcast live video and interact with audiences at scale without requiring attendees to download any software. The company has stayed a small, lean team of fewer than fifteen people and has raised a modest amount of outside capital, including a $120,000 seed round in December 2017 from investor NFX. Rather than chasing venture-scale growth, Crowdcast has positioned itself as a profitable, founder-led niche player serving thousands of course creators, membership businesses, and event organizers, reporting several million dollars in annual revenue in recent years. Architecturally, Crowdcast runs entirely in the browser using WebRTC-based live streaming, which means hosts and attendees join sessions through a link with no app installation required on desktop or mobile. The platform supports up to several presenters on camera at once, screen sharing, and simultaneous multistreaming to destinations like YouTube and Facebook Live on higher-tier plans. Sessions can run as one-off live shows, recurring webinar series, multi-day virtual summits, or cohort-based online courses, and every broadcast is automatically recorded and stored as an on-demand replay that can continue generating registrations and revenue long after the live event ends. Interactivity is central to the product: hosts can run live polls, moderate a persistent chat and Q&A queue, spotlight audience members on camera, and use calls-to-action that convert viewers into customers in real time. Monetization is built directly into the platform rather than bolted on through a separate checkout tool. Hosts can sell paid tickets to individual events, sell subscriptions or memberships that unlock a library of past and future events, and collect payments directly through the platform's integrated payment processing, with Crowdcast taking a percentage-based transaction fee that decreases on higher-priced plans. This makes Crowdcast a common choice for online course creators, authors, consultants, and membership community operators who need registration, live delivery, and monetization in a single tool rather than stitching together Zoom, Eventbrite, and a landing page builder. Crowdcast's pricing is structured around three self-serve monthly tiers plus a free 14-day trial capped at 60-minute sessions and ten live attendees. The Lite plan starts at $49 per month and supports 100 or more live attendees, ten hours of streaming per month, one team seat, 1 GB of Studio storage, and two-hour session limits, with a 5% transaction fee on paid tickets. The Pro plan, at $89 per month, raises the attendee cap to 250 or more, doubles streaming hours to twenty, adds a second team seat, 5 GB of storage, four-hour sessions, one multistream destination, and custom registration fields. The Business plan, at $195 per month, supports 1,000 or more live attendees, forty hours of monthly streaming, four team seats, 10 GB of storage, six-hour sessions, three simultaneous multistream destinations, and a reduced 2% transaction fee. Customers who commit to annual billing receive roughly a 30% discount versus paying month to month. Add-on costs include extra team seats at $29 each, additional streaming hours at $20 per five-hour block, and overage fees of $0.15 per attendee beyond a plan's cap. Crowdcast differentiates itself from general-purpose video conferencing tools like Zoom by focusing specifically on one-to-many broadcast experiences with monetization and audience engagement as first-class features rather than afterthoughts, and it differentiates itself from pure webinar tools like WebinarJam or Demio by emphasizing a more social, talk-show-style format with audience interaction and evergreen replay value. Its closest competitors include Livestorm, StreamYard, Demio, WebinarJam, Hopin-style summit tools, and Zoom Events, though Crowdcast tends to be favored by solo creators and small teams over large enterprise event marketing departments. Target users are online educators, authors and speakers, business coaches, community and membership platform operators, and B2B marketers running lead-generation webinars who want an all-in-one, no-download experience for their audience. Notable practical strengths repeatedly cited by users include the frictionless no-download join experience for attendees, the ability to monetize a single event or an entire library of replays, and the simplicity of the host dashboard compared to more complex production-oriented streaming tools. Commonly cited limitations include session length caps tied to plan tier, per-transaction fees that can add up for high-ticket events, and a smaller integration ecosystem than larger competitors backed by significantly more venture funding. Because Crowdcast has remained a small, independently operated company rather than raising large venture rounds, its roadmap has tended to prioritize steady refinement of its core webinar and monetization workflows over rapid feature expansion into adjacent categories like video conferencing or asynchronous video messaging, which is a trade-off some customers view as a strength, since the product stays focused, while others view it as a limitation compared with faster-moving, better-funded competitors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Crowdcast is a live video platform designed for hosting webinars, online courses, and virtual summits directly in the browser, with no software download required for hosts or attendees. Founded in 2015 by Sai Hossain, the company has grown as a lean, founder-led business rather than a venture-fueled hypergrowth startup.

The platform combines live broadcasting, audience interaction, and monetization tools in one product, making it popular with course creators, coaches, and membership businesses who want to avoid stitching together separate video, registration, and payment tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Every Crowdcast session runs on WebRTC streaming in the browser and is automatically recorded, turning live events into an evergreen library of on-demand replays that hosts can continue to sell or use for lead generation.

Built-in interactivity tools include live polls, moderated Q&A queues, audience spotlighting, and persistent chat, while integrated ticketing and subscription billing let hosts charge for access without a separate checkout tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Crowdcast offers a 14-day free trial, then three monthly plans: Lite at $49/month, Pro at $89/month, and Business at $195/month, each raising attendee caps, streaming hours, team seats, and storage while lowering the percentage-based transaction fee on paid tickets.
Annual billing saves roughly 30% compared to monthly pricing, and teams can add extra seats or streaming hours a la carte if they outgrow their plan's included allotment.

faqs.question: Do attendees need to download an app to join a Crowdcast event?
faqs.answer: No. Crowdcast runs entirely in the browser using WebRTC, so hosts and attendees join through a link without installing any software.

faqs.question: Can I charge for access to a Crowdcast event?
faqs.answer: Yes. Crowdcast has built-in ticketing and subscription billing so hosts can charge for individual events or ongoing access, with Crowdcast taking a percentage-based transaction fee that varies by plan.

faqs.question: Does Crowdcast offer a free plan?
faqs.answer: Crowdcast does not have a permanent free plan, but it offers a 14-day free trial limited to 60-minute sessions and up to ten live attendees.

faqs.question: What happens to a Crowdcast session after it ends?
faqs.answer: Sessions are automatically recorded and become on-demand replays that hosts can keep available to registrants or sell as evergreen content.

faqs.question: How is Crowdcast priced?
faqs.answer: Crowdcast uses three monthly plans, Lite at $49, Pro at $89, and Business at $195, which scale attendee limits, streaming hours, storage, and team seats, with roughly 30% savings on annual billing.

faqs.question: What is Crowdcast best suited for?
faqs.answer: Crowdcast is best suited for online course creators, coaches, authors, and membership communities that want to combine live broadcasting, audience interaction, and monetization in a single tool.

enrichment.short_description: CrowdSec is an open-source, crowdsourced intrusion detection and threat intelligence platform that shares real-time malicious IP data across its global community.
enrichment.seo_title: CrowdSec Review 2026: Crowdsourced Threat Intel & Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: CrowdSec is an open-source, crowdsourced IDS that blocks malicious IPs using threat data shared by a global community. See features, pricing, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CrowdSec is an open-source, crowdsourced cybersecurity platform that detects and blocks malicious IP addresses by pooling real-time attack signals from a global network of users. The company was founded in January 2020 by Philippe Humeau, Laurent Soubrevilla, and Thibault Koechlin, and is headquartered in Montrouge, a suburb of Paris, France, with a team of roughly 35 to 40 employees as of 2026. CrowdSec has raised more than $21 million in total funding, including a €14 million (approximately $14 million) Series A round announced in October 2022, positioning it as a well-capitalized European cybersecurity startup competing in the threat intelligence and intrusion prevention space. The core idea behind CrowdSec is participative security: when one organization running the CrowdSec Security Engine detects an attack, such as a brute-force login attempt, a web scraping bot, or an exploit scan, that signal is anonymized and shared with the wider CrowdSec network, and in return every other participant receives an updated blocklist reflecting attacks seen anywhere in the community. This crowdsourced model is explicitly modeled on the idea of a neighborhood watch for the internet, and CrowdSec markets itself as the first crowdsourced cyber threat intelligence (CTI) solution of its kind. The core CrowdSec Security Engine is released under the MIT open-source license and can be self-hosted for free, installed on Linux servers, containers, or edge devices to analyze logs in real time using configurable detection scenarios. Detected threats trigger local remediation through modular components called bouncers, which integrate with firewalls (iptables, nftables, pf), reverse proxies and web servers (Nginx, Apache, Traefik, HAProxy), CDNs, WordPress, and cloud provider security groups to automatically block or challenge malicious traffic. CrowdSec has reported that its software has been installed more than 100,000 times across roughly 175 countries, with users ranging from individual server administrators and small businesses to governments, e-commerce companies, and financial institutions. Beyond the free, self-hosted core engine, CrowdSec runs a hosted management layer called the CrowdSec Console, which gives administrators a centralized dashboard to monitor alerts, manage multiple Security Engines, and configure remediation policies across a fleet of servers; this is offered under a free Community tier as well as a paid Console Premium tier priced on a pay-as-you-grow basis per enrolled Security Engine, adding features like the Premium Community Blocklist, surge alert notifications, extended alert retention, and multi-channel notification integrations. CrowdSec also monetizes its aggregated threat data directly: its Platinum Blocklists product, aimed at network and infrastructure teams that want to block malicious traffic without running the full engine, starts at roughly $1,900 per month for small and midsize businesses and includes industry-specific, technology-specific, and country-specific blocklists as well as a dedicated AI-crawler blocklist, with simple integrations for common firewalls and CDNs. Separately, CrowdSec offers an IP Reputation and Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) API for developers who want to enrich their own security tooling with contextual data on IP addresses, starting around $49 per month for 5,000 queries and scaling up to bespoke enterprise API quotas and local, on-premises replication of the CTI dataset starting near $9,000 per month for organizations with strict latency or data residency requirements. The CTI product returns more than 30 contextual criteria per IP, including industry and country targeting, connection type, MITRE ATT&CK technique classification, and hourly updated behavioral scoring. CrowdSec differentiates itself from traditional, single-organization intrusion prevention tools like Fail2ban by turning detection into a collective, real-time defense network rather than something each server has to learn on its own, and it differentiates itself from commercial threat intelligence vendors like Recorded Future or Spamhaus by being open source at its core and driven by community-contributed signals rather than proprietary honeypots alone. Typical users include DevOps and platform engineering teams protecting self-managed infrastructure, managed service providers securing client environments, SaaS companies wanting to reduce credential-stuffing and bot traffic, and security teams that want to complement existing firewalls and WAFs with continuously updated, crowdsourced blocklists. Its open-core business model, in which the detection engine is free and open source while the console, premium blocklists, and CTI API are commercial, mirrors the approach used by other successful open-source security and infrastructure companies, and it allows CrowdSec to grow its user base and its collective threat data simultaneously, since every new free user who deploys the engine strengthens the blocklists that paying customers rely on. CrowdSec also maintains a public hub of community-contributed detection scenarios, parsers, and collections tailored to specific applications and services, letting users extend the engine to protect additional software without writing detection logic from scratch, which further reinforces the community-driven nature of the project. CrowdSec's growth strategy has relied heavily on this community flywheel: because the free Security Engine is genuinely useful on its own for blocking common attacks, the company has been able to build a large installed base first and monetize a smaller share of that base through premium blocklists, the CTI API, and enterprise console features, an approach that has helped it expand into 175-plus countries without the marketing spend a purely closed-source competitor would typically require.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: CrowdSec is an open-source intrusion detection and prevention platform that turns attack data from its global user base into a shared, real-time blocklist of malicious IP addresses. Founded in 2020 in France by Philippe Humeau, Laurent Soubrevilla, and Thibault Koechlin, the company built its Security Engine as a free, MIT-licensed alternative to running detection in isolation.

When one deployment detects an attack such as a brute-force attempt or a scraping bot, the anonymized signal is shared across the network so every participant benefits from what any other participant has already seen, creating a crowdsourced neighborhood watch for internet infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The free, self-hosted Security Engine parses logs using configurable detection scenarios and hands off enforcement to modular bouncers that integrate with firewalls, Nginx, Apache, WordPress, and cloud security groups.

The hosted Console gives teams centralized visibility across many enrolled engines, while the paid CTI API and Platinum Blocklists expose CrowdSec's aggregated threat data for teams that want IP reputation scoring without deploying the full engine.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: CrowdSec's Security Engine and Community Console tier are free to use. Paid options layer on top: Console Premium is billed per enrolled Security Engine on a pay-as-you-grow basis, the CTI/IP Reputation API starts near $49/month for 5,000 queries, and Platinum Blocklists start around $1,900/month for SMB customers.
Enterprise customers needing on-premises threat data replication for latency or compliance reasons can access local CTI synchronization starting near $9,000/month.

faqs.question: Is CrowdSec free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. The core CrowdSec Security Engine and the Community Console tier are free and open source under the MIT license. Paid tiers add premium blocklists, a CTI API, and enterprise support.

faqs.question: How does CrowdSec's crowdsourced detection work?
faqs.answer: When a CrowdSec deployment detects an attack, an anonymized signal is shared with the CrowdSec network, and every other participant's blocklist is updated with that malicious IP, creating a collective defense.

faqs.question: What is a CrowdSec bouncer?
faqs.answer: A bouncer is a remediation component that enforces blocking decisions made by the Security Engine, integrating with tools like firewalls, Nginx, Apache, and WordPress.

faqs.question: Can CrowdSec replace a traditional firewall?
faqs.answer: No. CrowdSec is designed to complement existing firewalls and WAFs by adding crowdsourced detection and blocking, not to replace network perimeter security entirely.

faqs.question: Who uses CrowdSec?
faqs.answer: CrowdSec is used by individual server administrators, DevOps teams, managed service providers, and larger organizations including governments and financial institutions, with reported installs exceeding 100,000 across roughly 175 countries.

faqs.question: What is the difference between CrowdSec and Fail2ban?
faqs.answer: Fail2ban blocks attackers based only on what a single server observes, while CrowdSec shares detection signals across its whole user community so every participant benefits from attacks detected elsewhere.

enrichment.short_description: CrowdStrike Falcon is a cloud-native cybersecurity platform combining endpoint protection, XDR, identity security, and threat intelligence in a single lightweight agent.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: CrowdStrike Falcon is a cloud-native EDR and XDR security platform with a single lightweight agent. Compare Falcon Go, Pro, Enterprise, and Complete pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CrowdStrike Falcon is the flagship cybersecurity platform of CrowdStrike Holdings, Inc., a publicly traded company (Nasdaq: CRWD) founded in 2011 by George Kurtz, Dmitri Alperovitch, and Gregg Marston. CrowdStrike launched the Falcon platform's first product, a next-generation antivirus offering, in June 2013, and has since expanded it into a full extended detection and response (XDR) ecosystem. The company was originally headquartered in Sunnyvale, California, and relocated its headquarters to Austin, Texas, in December 2021; it now employs more than 10,000 people worldwide and is one of the largest pure-play cybersecurity vendors by market capitalization. Architecturally, Falcon is built around a single lightweight software agent installed on endpoints, servers, and cloud workloads, which streams telemetry to CrowdStrike's cloud-native backend for real-time analysis rather than relying on periodic signature updates or on-device scanning engines. That cloud backend, historically branded the Threat Graph, correlates trillions of security events per week using AI and machine learning models trained partly on intelligence gathered by CrowdStrike's threat-hunting and incident-response teams, who investigate real-world breaches for enterprise and government clients. On top of this single-agent architecture, CrowdStrike has built more than 30 modules covering next-generation antivirus (NGAV), endpoint detection and response (EDR), extended detection and response (XDR) across email, identity, and cloud, cloud workload and container protection (CNAPP), identity threat detection and response, next-generation SIEM and log management (originally built on the Falcon LogScale/Humio acquisition), managed threat hunting, and an AI security analyst assistant called Charlotte AI that can summarize incidents and recommend or automate response actions. CrowdStrike sells Falcon through a tiered bundle structure rather than a fully modular a la carte model, so most customers choose a package that already includes the mix of modules appropriate to their maturity level and then add specialized modules such as cloud security or identity protection as needed. The entry-level Falcon Go bundle is aimed at small businesses with up to 100 devices and costs $7.99 per device per month billed monthly, or $59.99 per device billed annually, and includes next-generation antivirus, device control, and mobile device protection. Falcon Pro adds firewall management and full endpoint detection and response for $14.99 per device monthly or $99.99 annually. Falcon Enterprise, aimed at larger organizations that need proactive threat hunting and deeper investigation tooling, costs $19.99 per device monthly or $184.99 annually and adds threat intelligence and hunting capabilities on top of Pro. At the top of the range, Falcon Complete is a fully managed, human-led service in which a dedicated CrowdStrike team monitors, investigates, and remediates threats around the clock on the customer's behalf, often backed by a breach prevention warranty; pricing for Falcon Complete and other advanced modules such as cloud security, identity protection, and next-gen SIEM is custom-quoted, and typical full-platform enterprise deployments commonly range from roughly $60,000 to $180,000 or more per year depending on device count and module mix. CrowdStrike differentiates itself from legacy antivirus vendors and many competitors by using a single unified agent rather than requiring separate agents for endpoint protection, EDR, and other capabilities, which reduces performance overhead and simplifies deployment, and by combining its own telemetry with human-driven threat intelligence from its adversary-tracking research team, which names and tracks specific threat actor groups. Its most direct competitors include Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, SentinelOne, Palo Alto Networks Cortex XDR, Sophos Intercept X, and Trend Micro, and it is frequently evaluated head-to-head with Microsoft and SentinelOne in enterprise security bake-offs. Typical Falcon customers are mid-market and enterprise IT and security operations teams, managed security service providers, and regulated industries such as finance, healthcare, and government that need continuous endpoint visibility and rapid incident response. A significant event in the company's history occurred in July 2024, when a faulty content update to the Falcon sensor caused widespread crashes on Windows systems globally, disrupting airlines, hospitals, banks, and other critical infrastructure in one of the largest IT outages in recent memory; CrowdStrike has since publicly detailed remediation and process changes made in response. Despite that incident, CrowdStrike has continued to be recognized as a leader in independent analyst evaluations of endpoint protection and XDR platforms, and the Falcon platform remains one of the most widely deployed enterprise EDR/XDR solutions on the market. Beyond the core endpoint and identity modules, CrowdStrike has expanded Falcon into adjacent categories through both internal development and acquisitions, including cloud security posture management, exposure management and attack surface monitoring, and next-generation SIEM capabilities that let security teams consolidate log data from endpoints, cloud services, and third-party tools into a single analytics workspace rather than maintaining a separate legacy SIEM product. The company also operates CrowdStrike Falcon Intelligence, a threat intelligence service that publishes detailed profiles of named adversary groups (tracked using CrowdStrike's own naming taxonomy, such as designating nation-state and criminal actors by animal-themed labels tied to their country or motivation), which feeds directly into the detections and hunting playbooks available inside the Falcon platform. This combination of a single unified agent, a large and growing module marketplace, and human-curated threat intelligence is central to how CrowdStrike positions itself against competitors that assembled their platforms primarily through acquisition of disparate point products.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: CrowdStrike Falcon is a cloud-native cybersecurity platform built around a single lightweight agent that streams endpoint telemetry to CrowdStrike's cloud backend for real-time detection and response. Founded in 2011 by George Kurtz, Dmitri Alperovitch, and Gregg Marston, CrowdStrike launched Falcon's first product in 2013 and is now headquartered in Austin, Texas, as a publicly traded company with more than 10,000 employees.

Falcon has grown from an antivirus replacement into a broad security platform spanning endpoint protection, extended detection and response, identity threat protection, cloud workload security, and next-generation SIEM, delivered through more than 30 modules layered on top of the same base agent.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The single-agent architecture avoids the performance overhead of running separate tools for antivirus, EDR, and log collection, while CrowdStrike's cloud backend correlates telemetry across all customers using AI models informed by its own threat-hunting and incident-response investigations.

The Charlotte AI security analyst can summarize alerts and recommend or automate response steps, and CrowdStrike's managed Falcon Complete service adds a dedicated human team that monitors and remediates threats around the clock.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: CrowdStrike sells Falcon in tiered bundles: Falcon Go starts at $7.99/device/month ($59.99 annually) for small businesses up to 100 devices, Falcon Pro is $14.99/month ($99.99 annually) adding firewall management and EDR, and Falcon Enterprise is $19.99/month ($184.99 annually) adding threat hunting and intelligence.

Falcon Complete, the fully managed detection and response tier, along with advanced modules like cloud security and identity protection, is custom-quoted, and typical enterprise deployments across the full platform commonly total tens of thousands of dollars per year or more.

faqs.question: What is CrowdStrike Falcon?
faqs.answer: CrowdStrike Falcon is a cloud-native cybersecurity platform that uses a single lightweight agent to deliver antivirus, endpoint detection and response, identity protection, cloud security, and more.

faqs.question: How much does CrowdStrike Falcon cost?
faqs.answer: Falcon Go starts at $7.99 per device per month ($59.99 billed annually), Falcon Pro is $14.99/month ($99.99 annually), and Falcon Enterprise is $19.99/month ($184.99 annually); Falcon Complete and advanced modules are custom-quoted.

faqs.question: What is Falcon Complete?
faqs.answer: Falcon Complete is CrowdStrike's fully managed detection and response service, where a dedicated CrowdStrike team monitors, investigates, and remediates threats on the customer's behalf around the clock.

faqs.question: How many devices can Falcon Go support?
faqs.answer: Falcon Go is limited to a maximum of 100 devices, making it suitable primarily for small businesses; larger organizations need Falcon Pro or Enterprise.

faqs.question: What is Charlotte AI?
faqs.answer: Charlotte AI is CrowdStrike's generative AI security analyst that summarizes alerts and can recommend or automate incident response actions within the Falcon platform.

faqs.question: Who are CrowdStrike Falcon's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Falcon's closest competitors include Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, SentinelOne, Palo Alto Networks Cortex XDR, Sophos Intercept X, and Trend Micro.

enrichment.short_description: CryptPad is an open-source, end-to-end encrypted office suite offering documents, spreadsheets, kanban boards, whiteboards, and forms with zero-knowledge privacy.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: CryptPad is an open-source, end-to-end encrypted office suite for docs, sheets, and forms. See features, self-hosting options, and CryptPad pricing plans.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CryptPad is an open-source, end-to-end encrypted collaboration suite that provides rich text documents, spreadsheets, presentations, a code and markdown editor, kanban boards, a whiteboard, forms, and cloud file storage, all designed so that the server operator never has access to readable user data. The project originated from work by Caleb James De Lisle, an engineer formerly employed by XWiki SAS, a Paris-based company that has built open-source software for more than two decades. CryptPad grew out of a research grant that XWiki SAS received from BPI France to explore real-time collaborative editing, and De Lisle developed ChainPad, a conflict-free replicated data type (CRDT) based algorithm that allows multiple users to edit a document simultaneously while all synchronization data remains encrypted before it ever leaves the browser. Today CryptPad is maintained by a dedicated team inside XWiki SAS, informally called the CryptPad Squad, led by David Benque, with roughly seven core team members as of recent years. Technically, CryptPad's defining characteristic is its zero-knowledge architecture: encryption and decryption happen entirely client-side in the user's browser using keys derived from the user's credentials, so the server that hosts and synchronizes documents only ever stores and transmits encrypted ciphertext. This means CryptPad administrators, including the operators of the flagship public instance, cannot read document contents, see file names, or access account passwords even if compelled to, which is a meaningful differentiator from mainstream office suites like Google Workspace or Microsoft 365, where the provider technically has access to unencrypted content on its servers. CryptPad is released as free and open-source software, with its source code hosted publicly on GitHub, and it can be self-hosted at no cost by any individual or organization with the technical capacity to run a Node.js-based server, giving privacy-conscious users, activists, journalists, and organizations subject to strict data-sovereignty requirements full control over where their data lives. For users who do not want to self-host, XWiki operates a flagship public instance at cryptpad.fr offering free accounts with baseline storage, plus optional individual paid plans ranging from about €5 to €100 per month for additional storage and priority support. Organizations that want a managed or dedicated deployment can purchase CryptPad Enterprise plans, which start around €1,500 per year and scale through several tiers: a Mini tier for 50 users and 100 GB of storage at roughly €3,000 per year, a Small tier for 250 users and 200 GB at about €6,000 per year, a Medium tier for 500 users and 500 GB at about €12,500 per year, and a Large tier for 1,000 users and 1 TB at about €25,000 per year, with all enterprise tiers including SSO integration, managed or on-premise hosting, and support; education and nonprofit organizations receive a 50% discount on these enterprise rates. This layered model, free open-source core plus paid hosting and support, mirrors the open-core approach used by many privacy-focused infrastructure companies and allows CryptPad to sustain development while keeping the underlying software freely available to anyone. CryptPad has gained particular attention in public-sector and privacy-sensitive contexts; reporting in 2025 noted that United Nations personnel had begun piloting CryptPad as an alternative to Google Workspace for internal collaboration, reflecting growing institutional interest in sovereign, encrypted alternatives to major US cloud office suites, particularly within European public administrations and organizations bound by GDPR or similar data-protection regimes. CryptPad's most direct competitors include Nextcloud (particularly Nextcloud Office/Collabora), Cryptee, Skiff (before its 2024 shutdown and acquisition by Notion), Proton Drive and Proton Docs, and, at a broader level, mainstream suites like Google Workspace and Microsoft 365 that CryptPad positions itself as a privacy-respecting alternative to. Typical CryptPad users include privacy advocates, journalists and NGOs handling sensitive information, educational institutions, government agencies with data-sovereignty mandates, and small teams or open-source projects that want collaborative documents without depending on a US-based cloud provider. Because CryptPad is open source, self-hostable, and does not rely on advertising or data monetization for revenue, its business model depends on a mix of donations, paid hosting on the flagship instance, and enterprise contracts, which keeps its incentives aligned with user privacy rather than data collection. The project also publishes its source code publicly and accepts community contributions, which allows independent security researchers to audit its cryptographic implementation rather than trusting the encryption claims of a closed-source vendor, an assurance that is particularly important for organizations subject to strict compliance or data-protection obligations. CryptPad additionally supports fine-grained sharing controls, including view-only and edit links with expiration options, and password-protected access to individual documents, so teams can collaborate with external parties without granting them a full account or broader access to a shared workspace. Because CryptPad is developed by an established open-source company rather than an anonymous or purely volunteer-run project, it benefits from more predictable release cycles and long-term maintenance commitments than many smaller privacy tools, which is one reason institutional adopters such as public administrations have been willing to evaluate it as a genuine production alternative to mainstream commercial office suites rather than treating it purely as an experimental or hobbyist tool.

enrichment.short_description: Cube is an open-core semantic layer platform that defines metrics and access rules once and exposes them via SQL, REST, and GraphQL APIs for BI, apps, and AI agents.
enrichment.seo_title: Cube Review 2026: Semantic Layer Features & Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cube is an open-source, headless semantic layer for BI, embedded analytics, and AI agents. Compare Cube Core vs Cube Cloud pricing, features, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cube is a semantic layer platform that lets data teams define metrics, dimensions, joins, and access-control rules once in code and then expose that governed data model to any downstream consumer, business intelligence tools, embedded analytics inside customer-facing applications, spreadsheets, and, increasingly, AI agents and large language models, through SQL, REST, and GraphQL APIs. The company behind Cube, Cube Dev, Inc., was founded in 2016 by Artyom Keydunov and Pavel Tiunov and is headquartered in San Francisco, California. The product began as Cube.js, a JavaScript framework originally built out of an earlier project called Statsbot before the founders pivoted specifically toward the semantic layer category, and it has since been rebranded simply as Cube. Cube Dev has raised a total of roughly $47 million across three funding rounds, including a $25 million round that included Databricks as a strategic investor and partner, reflecting the growing interest of large data-platform vendors in the semantic-layer space as a neutral governance layer that sits between raw data warehouses and the tools people actually use to query that data. Architecturally, Cube is split into an open-source core and a commercial cloud product. Cube Core is released under a permissive license, Apache 2.0 for the backend server and MIT for portions of the client libraries, is available on GitHub with roughly 20,000 stars, and can be self-hosted for free. Cube Core lets engineers define a data model in code (YAML or JavaScript/TypeScript) describing metrics, dimensions, pre-aggregations, and row- or column-level access policies, connects to virtually any SQL data warehouse or database (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift, Postgres, ClickHouse, Databricks, and dozens of others), and then serves that model out through a REST API, a GraphQL API, and a Postgres-compatible SQL API that lets existing BI tools like Tableau, Power BI, and Looker Studio connect to Cube as if it were a database. Cube Cloud is the company's managed, enterprise-grade product, adding a hosted semantic layer IDE, Cube Store (a specialized caching and pre-aggregation engine that dramatically speeds up query performance), workbooks and dashboards for exploring the semantic layer directly, agentic analytics features that let AI copilots and chat interfaces query governed metrics rather than raw tables (reducing the risk of an LLM hallucinating incorrect numbers), production orchestration, monitoring, and role-based access controls. Cube's central positioning in the market is as a headless, API-first semantic layer, meaning it deliberately does not ship its own dashboarding or visualization front end, instead focusing on being the single governed source of metric definitions that any number of downstream BI tools, internal apps, or AI systems can share, which avoids the common problem of different teams calculating the same metric differently in different tools. Cube's pricing follows a freemium, open-core structure. The Free tier of Cube Cloud is free forever and aimed at hobbyists and small projects, including data source connections, the semantic layer IDE, workbooks, and dashboards. The Starter tier costs $40 per developer per month plus usage-based consumption billed in Cube Compute Units at roughly $0.10 per unit, with a $99 per month minimum, and adds extended agent limits, premium LLM access, unlimited workbooks, and production deployment. The Premium tier costs $80 per developer per month plus consumption at roughly $0.25 per Cube Compute Unit with a $10,000 annual commitment, and adds Explorer and Viewer user roles, embedded dashboards and analytics chat, unlimited queries, a 99.95% uptime SLA, custom domains, and multi-cluster deployment; additional Explorer seats cost $40 per user per month and Viewer seats cost $20 per user per month on Premium and above. The Enterprise tier is custom-priced and adds a 99.99% uptime SLA, dedicated single-tenant infrastructure, bring-your-own-cloud (BYOC) deployment, custom LLM integration, SSO/SAML, granular workspace access controls, and a DAX API specifically for connecting Cube to Microsoft Power BI. Cube's primary competitors include dbt Labs' Semantic Layer (built on the MetricFlow engine), Looker's LookML modeling layer from Google Cloud, AtScale, and Kyligence, and it is frequently discussed as an alternative or complement to building a custom metrics layer by hand inside a data warehouse. Typical Cube users are data engineering and analytics engineering teams at software companies that need to embed analytics or dashboards inside their own product for customers, as well as data platform teams at larger organizations that want a single governed definition of business metrics shared consistently across Tableau, Power BI, internal tools, and, more recently, AI chat assistants and agents that need to query trustworthy numbers rather than raw, ungoverned tables. Cube publishes extensive documentation and maintains an active open-source community around Cube Core, and the company has positioned the rise of AI copilots and natural-language analytics as a major tailwind for the semantic-layer category generally, arguing that a governed metrics layer becomes even more important once an LLM, rather than a human analyst who can sanity-check a number, is the one writing and executing queries against a company's data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Cube is a headless, API-first semantic layer that lets data teams define metrics, dimensions, and access rules once and serve them consistently to BI tools, embedded analytics, and AI agents. Founded in 2016 by Artyom Keydunov and Pavel Tiunov and headquartered in San Francisco, the company evolved from the earlier Cube.js JavaScript framework into a dedicated semantic-layer platform.

Cube deliberately does not ship its own visualization front end. Instead it focuses on being the single source of governed metric definitions that Tableau, Power BI, internal apps, and AI copilots can all query consistently through SQL, REST, and GraphQL APIs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Cube Core is open source (Apache 2.0 backend, MIT client libraries) with roughly 20,000 GitHub stars, and connects to most major SQL warehouses and databases while exposing a Postgres-compatible SQL API alongside REST and GraphQL endpoints.

Cube Cloud, the managed commercial product, adds Cube Store caching and pre-aggregations for performance, a semantic layer IDE, embedded dashboards, and agentic analytics support so AI copilots query governed metrics instead of raw, ungoverned tables.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Cube Cloud offers a free-forever tier for small projects, a Starter tier at $40 per developer per month plus usage-based Cube Compute Unit consumption ($99/month minimum), and a Premium tier at $80 per developer per month plus consumption with a $10,000 annual commitment.

Enterprise pricing is custom and adds a 99.99% SLA, single-tenant and bring-your-own-cloud deployment, custom LLM integration, SSO/SAML, and a DAX API for Power BI connectivity; the fully open-source Cube Core remains free to self-host indefinitely.

faqs.question: What is a semantic layer, and what does Cube do?
faqs.answer: A semantic layer defines business metrics, dimensions, and access rules once so every tool that queries the data gets consistent results. Cube implements this as a headless platform accessible via SQL, REST, and GraphQL APIs.

faqs.question: Is Cube open source?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cube Core, the underlying engine, is open source under Apache 2.0 (backend) and MIT (client) licenses and can be self-hosted for free. Cube Cloud is the company's paid, managed version.

faqs.question: How is Cube priced?
faqs.answer: Cube Cloud has a free tier, a Starter tier at $40/developer/month plus usage-based consumption, a Premium tier at $80/developer/month plus consumption with a $10K/year commitment, and custom Enterprise pricing.

faqs.question: Can Cube connect to my existing BI tool?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cube exposes a Postgres-compatible SQL API, so tools like Tableau, Power BI, and Looker Studio can connect to it as if it were a regular database, in addition to REST and GraphQL APIs.

faqs.question: Is Cube designed for AI use cases?
faqs.answer: Cube markets itself as an agentic analytics platform, letting AI copilots and LLM-based agents query governed metric definitions instead of raw tables, which reduces the risk of incorrect or hallucinated figures.

faqs.question: Who are Cube's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Cube's closest competitors include dbt Labs' Semantic Layer (MetricFlow), Looker's LookML modeling layer, AtScale, and Kyligence.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Culture Amp is an employee experience platform for engagement surveys and performance management. See features, pricing tiers, and top alternatives for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Culture Amp is an employee experience platform used by human resources and people teams to run engagement surveys, manage performance reviews and goal-setting, and turn employee feedback into people analytics that inform organizational decisions. The company was founded in 2009 in Melbourne, Australia, by Didier Elzinga, Doug English, Rod Hamilton, and Jon Williams, and it remains headquartered in Melbourne while operating additional offices in the United States (including San Francisco), the United Kingdom, and Germany to serve its global customer base. Culture Amp has raised approximately $258 million in total venture funding from investors including Sequoia Capital, Index Ventures, Blackbird Ventures, and Felicis Ventures, and reached unicorn status after closing a $150 million Series F round in July 2021 co-led by Sequoia Capital China and TDM Growth Partners, which valued the company at $1.5 billion. As of recent reporting, Culture Amp serves more than 6,500 companies and over 25 million employees worldwide, ranging from fast-growing technology startups to large multinational enterprises, making it one of the most widely adopted platforms in the employee engagement and performance management category. Culture Amp differentiates itself from generic survey tools by grounding its product in what it calls people science, drawing on organizational behavior research and industrial-organizational (I/O) psychology to design scientifically validated survey questions and provide benchmarking data across industries, company sizes, and regions so HR teams can compare their own engagement scores against relevant peer groups rather than guessing at what a good score looks like. The platform is organized around several core products that can be purchased individually or together: Engage, which covers employee engagement surveys, pulse surveys, and always-on feedback channels; Perform, which covers performance management workflows including goal-setting (OKRs), continuous feedback, 360-degree reviews, and structured performance review cycles; and Develop, an add-on focused on skills mapping and career development planning. Culture Amp also offers People Science Consulting as a premium add-on, giving customers direct access to Culture Amp's in-house researchers and organizational psychologists for strategic advisory work, and it has layered generative AI capabilities into the platform through a feature called AI Coach, which helps managers prepare for difficult conversations, summarize open-ended survey comments, and draft development plans. All Culture Amp plans share a baseline of enterprise-oriented capabilities, including single sign-on (SSO), data encryption, integrations with common HRIS platforms (such as Workday, BambooHR, and ADP), multilingual survey support for global workforces, and SOC 2 compliance for data security. Culture Amp does not publish specific list prices publicly; instead, pricing depends on the number of employees at the customer organization, which specific products are purchased (Engage alone, Perform alone, or the combined platform), and the service tier selected, with all contracts billed annually rather than monthly. The company segments its go-to-market and support model by company size: organizations under roughly 200 employees receive group-based Customer Success support with 24/5 chat and email access; mid-market organizations with 200 to 999 employees get a dedicated one-on-one Customer Success partnership along with expert-led implementation project management; and enterprise organizations with 1,000 or more employees receive additional services such as manager and executive training, one-on-one and group coaching, and dedicated support resources. Third-party pricing aggregators have estimated a starting price in the range of roughly $30 per employee per month for smaller deployments, though prospective customers must request a formal quote or book a demo with Culture Amp's sales team to get an accurate number for their organization. Culture Amp's primary competitors in the employee experience and engagement category include Lattice, which pairs performance reviews with engagement surveys in a single system; 15Five, known for continuous listening, manager enablement, and simpler pulse-survey workflows; Leapsome; Qualtrics EmployeeXM; and Microsoft Viva Glint, which has a natural advantage for organizations already standardized on the Microsoft 365 ecosystem. Typical Culture Amp customers are HR, People Operations, and Talent teams at mid-market and enterprise companies that want research-backed survey methodology, robust benchmarking data, and a combined engagement-plus-performance platform rather than piecing together separate point solutions for surveys and performance reviews. The company also publishes original research and thought leadership through its people science team, including periodic reports on workplace trends and benchmarking data, which it uses both as a marketing channel and as an input into how it designs and validates new survey questions and product features. Over time Culture Amp has broadened its platform beyond its original engagement-survey product into adjacent areas such as performance management and skills development, reflecting a strategy of becoming a single system that spans more of the employee lifecycle rather than remaining a point solution focused only on annual engagement surveys. Because Culture Amp sells to HR buyers who are themselves evaluated on measurable outcomes like retention and engagement scores, the company has emphasized case studies and outcome data in its marketing and sales process, positioning its research-backed methodology as a way for HR teams to justify programmatic investment in engagement and performance initiatives to their own executive leadership and boards.

Founded in 2009 in Melbourne, Australia, by Didier Elzinga, Doug English, Rod Hamilton, and Jon Williams, the company has grown into a global unicorn serving more than 6,500 companies and 25 million-plus employees.

Culture Amp reached a $1.5 billion valuation after a $150 million Series F round in July 2021 led by Sequoia Capital China and TDM Growth Partners, bringing its total funding to roughly $258 million from investors including Sequoia Capital, Index Ventures, and Blackbird Ventures.

Support and service levels scale with company size, from group-based Customer Success for organizations under 200 employees to dedicated one-on-one partnerships and executive coaching for enterprise customers with 1,000 or more employees; third-party estimates place entry pricing near $30 per employee per month for smaller deployments.

enrichment.short_description: CUPS (Common Unix Printing System) is a free, open-source printing system for Linux and Unix-like operating systems, using the Internet Printing Protocol.
enrichment.seo_title: CUPS: The Open-Source Unix Printing System Explained
enrichment.seo_meta_description: CUPS is the free, open-source Common Unix Printing System used by Linux, macOS, and Unix. Learn its history, architecture, licensing, and how it works.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CUPS, originally an acronym for the Common UNIX Printing System and now used simply as CUPS, is a free and open-source printing system that provides a standards-based printing architecture for Linux and other Unix-like operating systems. Development began in 1997 by Michael Sweet, who at the time owned a company called Easy Software Products, with the first public beta releases appearing in 1999. The project's original name was shortened to just CUPS starting with version 1.4 because of legal concerns over use of the trademarked term UNIX. Unlike its predecessor systems, which relied on the older Line Printer Daemon (LPD) protocol, CUPS was built around the Internet Printing Protocol (IPP), an HTTP-based standard that offered better support for modern network printers, richer printer capability discovery, and fewer vendor compatibility problems than LPD. This design choice helped CUPS become the de facto default printing system on nearly every major Linux distribution during the 2000s, replacing older printing subsystems like LPRng across the ecosystem. CUPS gained a second, very significant distribution channel in March 2002, when Apple Inc. adopted it as the underlying printing system for Mac OS X 10.2 (Jaguar), a role it has continued to play in every subsequent version of macOS. Apple's involvement deepened considerably in February 2007, when the company hired Michael Sweet, CUPS's chief developer, and formally acquired the CUPS source code and trademark, effectively bringing the project's primary development in-house at Apple while keeping it available under an open-source license. Apple continued to steward CUPS development for over a decade, until December 2019, when Michael Sweet announced he was leaving Apple. In 2020, stewardship of CUPS's upstream development transitioned to OpenPrinting, a community and industry organization focused on Linux and Unix printing standards, which now maintains the project's GitHub repository and drives ongoing feature work, including the CUPS 3.x generation of the software and related components such as libcups (the client-side CUPS API library), cups-local (a local server component for CUPS 3), and PAPPL, a framework for building modern IPP-based printer applications. CUPS is licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0, with an added exception clause that permits linking against software licensed only under GPLv2, a licensing approach shared by most of OpenPrinting's related projects. Architecturally, CUPS functions as a modular printing system composed of a background scheduler daemon (cupsd in the CUPS 2.x line, with CUPS 3.x moving toward a more distributed local-server model), a set of filters that convert documents from application-native formats into formats a given printer understands, backends that handle the actual transmission of print jobs to devices over USB, network IPP, or other connections, and a web-based administrative interface for configuring printers and monitoring queues. Applications and command-line tools interact with CUPS through its API and through standard Unix printing commands such as lp and lpr, both of which CUPS maintains for backward compatibility with older Unix printing conventions even though its internal protocol implementation is IPP-based. Because CUPS is free, open-source, and bundled by default with virtually every major Linux distribution as well as macOS, it does not have a commercial pricing model, a company headquarters, or a conventional employee count in the way a SaaS product would; it is best understood as critical, largely invisible infrastructure software maintained by a small, community-driven engineering effort under the OpenPrinting umbrella rather than as a company with a go-to-market or sales organization. There is no direct commercial competitor in the same sense as SaaS products, since CUPS effectively is the standard printing system for open-source operating systems, though it can be contrasted with proprietary printing subsystems built into Windows (the Windows Print Spooler) and with printer-manufacturer-specific driver software. For end users, CUPS is largely invisible, working behind the scenes whenever a Linux or macOS system prints a document, but for system administrators and developers it exposes a documented API, a web administration interface typically reachable on port 631, and configuration files that allow fine-grained control over shared printers, access permissions, and job queue management in both home and enterprise network environments. CUPS also underpins network printer discovery on Unix-like systems through protocols such as DNS-SD/Bonjour and IPP Everywhere, which lets modern printers advertise their capabilities on a local network so that client machines can add and configure them automatically without installing manufacturer-specific driver packages. This driverless printing model, sometimes referred to as universal printing, has become increasingly important as the printing industry has standardized around IPP, and it is one of the main areas of ongoing work in the CUPS 3.x generation of the software led by OpenPrinting, alongside improved support for containerized and sandboxed environments where a traditional system-wide print daemon is not always practical to run. Because CUPS predates almost every modern Linux distribution and desktop environment in its current form, it has also become a stable, well-understood integration point that printer manufacturers, distribution maintainers, and desktop projects like GNOME and KDE all build against, which is part of why it has remained the de facto standard rather than being displaced by a newer alternative despite periodic community discussion about modernizing the Unix printing stack.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: CUPS (Common Unix Printing System) is a free, open-source printing system that provides the standard printing architecture for Linux, macOS, and other Unix-like operating systems. Development began in 1997, led by Michael Sweet of Easy Software Products, with public betas arriving in 1999, and the project moved to using the Internet Printing Protocol (IPP) instead of the older Line Printer Daemon protocol.

Apple adopted CUPS as the printing system for Mac OS X 10.2 in March 2002 and later hired Michael Sweet and acquired the CUPS source code in February 2007. Since Sweet's departure from Apple in December 2019, upstream development has been stewarded by OpenPrinting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: CUPS uses the Internet Printing Protocol as its core transport, giving it richer printer discovery and better vendor compatibility than older LPD-based printing systems, while still supporting legacy lp and lpr commands for backward compatibility.

The system is modular, combining a scheduler daemon, document-conversion filters, device backends, and a web-based administrative interface, and is complemented by newer OpenPrinting components like libcups and PAPPL for building modern IPP printer applications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: CUPS is free and open source, licensed under Apache License 2.0 with a GPLv2 linking exception, and is bundled by default with virtually every major Linux distribution and macOS at no cost.

Because it is infrastructure software rather than a commercial SaaS product, there is no paid tier, subscription, or enterprise pricing plan; ongoing development is sustained by the OpenPrinting community rather than a commercial licensing model.

faqs.question: What does CUPS stand for?
faqs.answer: CUPS originally stood for the Common UNIX Printing System, though the name was shortened to just CUPS starting with version 1.4 due to trademark concerns over the term UNIX.

faqs.question: Who created CUPS?
faqs.answer: CUPS was created by Michael Sweet, who began development in 1997 through his company Easy Software Products, with the first public betas released in 1999.

faqs.question: Does Apple own CUPS?
faqs.answer: Apple acquired the CUPS source code and hired its chief developer, Michael Sweet, in February 2007, and used CUPS as macOS's printing system. Since Sweet left Apple in 2019, upstream development has been led by the OpenPrinting organization, though CUPS remains open source.

faqs.question: Is CUPS free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. CUPS is free and open-source software licensed under Apache License 2.0 with a GPLv2 linking exception, and it comes bundled by default with most Linux distributions and macOS.

faqs.question: What protocol does CUPS use to communicate with printers?
faqs.answer: CUPS uses the Internet Printing Protocol (IPP), an HTTP-based standard, rather than the older Line Printer Daemon (LPD) protocol used by earlier Unix printing systems.

faqs.question: Who maintains CUPS today?
faqs.answer: Since 2020, upstream development of CUPS has been maintained by OpenPrinting, a community and industry organization focused on Linux and Unix printing standards.

enrichment.short_description: Cursor is an AI-native code editor built by Anysphere that uses agentic AI to write, edit, and review code across a codebase from natural-language instructions.
enrichment.seo_title: Cursor AI Code Editor Review 2026: Features & Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cursor is Anysphere's AI-native code editor with agentic coding, Tab autocomplete, and Bugbot review. Compare Cursor's Hobby, Individual, and Teams pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cursor is an AI-native code editor built by Anysphere, Inc., a San Francisco-based software company founded in 2022 by MIT students and dropouts Michael Truell, Sualeh Asif, Aman Sanger, and Arvid Lunnemark, who left the company in October 2025. Cursor is built as a fork of Microsoft's open-source Visual Studio Code editor, which lets it retain compatibility with the existing VS Code extension ecosystem and keyboard shortcuts while layering deeply integrated, codebase-aware AI capabilities on top. Rather than treating AI assistance as a chat sidebar bolted onto a traditional editor, Cursor is designed around an agentic workflow in which the AI can read and search across an entire codebase, propose and apply multi-file edits, run terminal commands, and iterate on its own output based on test results or error messages, all initiated from natural-language instructions. Core product capabilities include Tab, a predictive, multi-line autocomplete feature that suggests entire edits rather than single lines; Agent (formerly and still sometimes called Composer), a more autonomous mode that can plan and execute multi-step coding tasks across many files with limited supervision; Bugbot, an automated code-review feature that flags likely bugs and issues in pull requests; and Cloud Agents, which let coding agents run asynchronously in the background rather than only inside the local editor session. Cursor supports the Model Context Protocol (MCP) for connecting external tools and data sources to its agents, and it gives users access to multiple frontier AI models from providers including Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google, alongside Anysphere's own in-house Composer model, rather than locking users into a single model provider. Anysphere's funding history has been unusually fast even by the standards of the current AI boom: after an $8 million seed round in October 2023 led by the OpenAI Startup Fund, the company raised a $60 million Series A in 2024 at roughly a $400 million valuation, then a $900 million Series C in June 2025 led by Thrive Capital at a $9.9 billion valuation, followed just months later by a $2.3 billion Series D in November 2025 co-led by Accel and Coatue that pushed its valuation to $29.3 billion. In early 2026 the company was reportedly in talks to raise more than $2 billion at a valuation above $50 billion with participation from Andreessen Horowitz, Thrive Capital, and Nvidia as a strategic investor. On June 16, 2026, SpaceX announced it would acquire Anysphere in an all-stock deal valuing the company at approximately $60 billion, with the transaction expected to close in the third quarter of 2026 and Cursor to operate as part of a SpaceXAI subsidiary; as of the announcement, the deal had not yet closed. Cursor's revenue growth has been described as the fastest ramp of any application-layer software company to date, reportedly crossing $100 million in annual recurring revenue in January 2025, surpassing $500 million by June 2025, exceeding $1 billion by November 2025, and passing $2 billion by February 2026. Cursor's pricing follows a freemium model with four tiers. The Hobby plan is free and offers limited Agent requests and limited Tab completions with no credit card required, aimed at developers trying the product casually. The Individual plan costs $16 per month and adds extended Agent limits, generous access to models like Grok and Composer, access to frontier models generally, support for MCP servers, skills, and hooks, cloud agents, and usage-based Bugbot billing. The Teams plan costs $32 per user per month and adds centralized team billing and administration, a team marketplace for sharing internal rules and plugins, agentic code review through Bugbot, cloud agents with shared team context, usage analytics, team-wide privacy mode, and SAML/OIDC single sign-on. The Enterprise plan is custom-priced and adds pooled usage across the organization, invoice and purchase-order billing, SCIM-based seat management, granular controls over repository, model, and MCP access, network and browser controls for agents, audit logging, service accounts, an AI code-tracking API, and priority support. In July 2025, Cursor faced user backlash after changing how its (then-named) Pro plan handled usage-based billing, leading to unexpectedly large charges for some customers; the company responded by adjusting its limits and issuing refunds. Cursor's primary competitors include GitHub Copilot, Windsurf (formerly Codeium), JetBrains AI Assistant, Amazon Q Developer, Replit, Zed, and Anthropic's own Claude Code command-line agent, and Cursor is generally considered one of the category-defining products in the AI-native code editor and coding-agent space that emerged in 2023 and 2024. Typical Cursor users are professional software engineers and engineering teams at startups and larger companies who want AI assistance embedded directly into their primary development environment rather than as a separate chat tool, ranging from individual developers on the free or Individual plan up to large engineering organizations on Enterprise contracts requiring centralized governance and audit controls. Cursor has also become closely associated with the broader shift in software development toward AI-assisted and increasingly autonomous coding workflows, with its rapid revenue growth frequently cited by industry analysts as evidence of how quickly professional developers have been willing to pay for AI tools that materially speed up day-to-day coding work rather than merely offering novelty chat assistance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Cursor is an AI-native code editor, forked from Visual Studio Code, built by Anysphere, Inc., a San Francisco company founded in 2022 by MIT students and dropouts Michael Truell, Sualeh Asif, Aman Sanger, and Arvid Lunnemark. Rather than bolting AI chat onto a traditional editor, Cursor is designed around agentic workflows where the AI can read across a codebase, edit multiple files, run terminal commands, and iterate based on test or error output.

Anysphere has grown at an unusually fast pace, moving from an $8 million seed round in October 2023 to a $29.3 billion valuation by November 2025, and in June 2026 SpaceX announced an agreement to acquire Anysphere in an all-stock deal valuing the company at roughly $60 billion, with the deal expected to close in Q3 2026.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core features include Tab, a predictive multi-line autocomplete; Agent (Composer), an autonomous multi-file coding mode; Bugbot, automated pull-request code review; and Cloud Agents, which let coding agents run asynchronously in the background rather than only in a live editor session.

Cursor supports the Model Context Protocol (MCP) for connecting external tools, and gives users access to multiple frontier AI models from Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google alongside Anysphere's own Composer model, rather than locking developers into a single provider.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Cursor offers a free Hobby tier with limited Agent and Tab usage, an Individual plan at $16 per month with extended limits and frontier model access, and a Teams plan at $32 per user per month adding centralized billing, Bugbot code review, and SSO.

Enterprise pricing is custom and adds pooled usage, SCIM provisioning, granular access controls, audit logs, and an AI code-tracking API; a July 2025 change to usage-based billing on the paid individual tier drew user complaints, which Cursor addressed with limit adjustments and refunds.
